preamble: The open vowel /a/ shifts to the close front vowel /i/ through a chain of raising and fronting processes. This shift occurs as part of larger vowel chain shifts, most famously in the Great Vowel Shift of English (15th-18th centuries), where /aː/ was raised through intermediate stages toward /iː/. The process involves both vowel height increase (lowering → raising) and frontal movement.

languageExamples.language: Middle English to Early Modern English (Great Vowel Shift)

examples.note: Part of the Great Vowel Shift: /a:/ raised to /æ:/, then further raising toward /e:/ and /i:/ occurred in chain shift dynamics
The long /a:/ was raised and fronted, eventually diphthongizing to [eɪ], reflecting the intermediate stages of a→i chain
Systematic raising of long /a:/ in early Middle English, part of broader vowel system restructuring

related.label: Intermediate raising step in chain shift
